claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child in care for 16 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children I'm asking about - Alex, who is 18, and Blake, who is 13.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you assess eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

Let me start by asking about your residency: **Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK at the moment. I'm living abroad.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since you're living abroad, there are some specific circumstances where you may still be able to claim Child Benefit.

**Which country are you currently living in, and are you (or your partner) a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of the armed forces posted abroad)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm living in France, and no, neither my partner nor I are Crown servants or in the armed forces or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

**Are you or your partner an EEA or Swiss national, or do you have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, we don't have any of those statuses - we're not EEA or Swiss nationals, and we don't have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me ask about your children's situations now.

**Starting with Alex (who is 18): Is Alex still in approved education or training (such as A-levels, college courses, or an approved training program)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They finished school and aren't studying anymore.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now let me ask about Blake.

**Does Blake (who is 13) live with you in France, or does Blake live in the UK with someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me here in France.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me also check one final detail:

**Do you or your partner contribute financially towards Blake's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, or housing) even though Blake is living with you in France?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, of course - Blake lives with me, so I provide for all their needs - food, clothes, housing, everything they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all the information. Based on your circumstances, I'm afraid you are **not eligible for Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake. Here's why:

**For Alex (18 years old):**
Alex does not meet the eligibility requirements because they are 18 and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16, or up to age 20 if they remain in approved education or training. Since Alex has finished school and isn't currently studying or in training, they don't qualify.

**For Blake (13 years old):**
Although Blake is under 16 and you're providing for all their needs, the residency requirement is not met. Child Benefit normally requires that you live in the UK. While there are exceptions for people living abroad—such as Crown servants (diplomats, armed forces personnel) or those with certain EU settlement statuses—you don't fall into any of these exceptional categories. Therefore, living in France without these qualifying circumstances means you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake.

I'm sorry this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If your circumstances change in the future (for example, if you move back to the UK, or if Alex returns to education), you may want to reassess your eligibility at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and is not in approved education or training. Under Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, or up to age 20 if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is not in approved education or training, they do not meet the age and education requirements for Child Benefit eligibility.

Blake is 13 years old and lives with the claimant in France. Under Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. The claimant does not live in the UK. According to the rules, you may be able to get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries or if you're a Crown servant. The claimant is not a Crown servant, and they do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, nor are they EEA or Swiss nationals with a right to reside. Therefore, Blake does not meet the residency requirements for Child Benefit eligibility.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is living in France with their two children, Alex (18) and Blake (13). Neither the claimant nor their partner are Crown servants or have any special status (settled/pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme, or EEA/Swiss national status) that would allow them to claim Child Benefit while living abroad. Alex does not meet the age requirements as they are 18 and not in approved education or training. Blake, while under 16, does not meet the residency requirements as the family lives in France and has no qualifying circumstances for claiming while abroad. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either child.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 18 years old and is not in approved education or training. Under Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, or up to age 20 if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is not in approved education or training, they do not meet the age and education requirements for Child Benefit eligibility.

Blake is 13 years old and lives with the claimant in France. Under Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. The claimant does not live in the UK. According to the rules, you may be able to get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries or if you're a Crown servant. The claimant is not a Crown servant, and they do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, nor are they EEA or Swiss nationals with a right to reside. Therefore, Blake does not meet the residency requirements for Child Benefit eligibility.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is living in France with their two children, Alex (18) and Blake (13). Neither the claimant nor their partner are Crown servants or have any special status (settled/pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme, or EEA/Swiss national status) that would allow them to claim Child Benefit while living abroad. Alex does not meet the age requirements as they are 18 and not in approved education or training. Blake, while under 16, does not meet the residency requirements as the family lives in France and has no qualifying circumstances for claiming while abroad. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either child.
